P000_S0010.quote:
ignoring the constraints from
$\mathcal{B}(B_c \to \tau \bar{\nu}_\tau)$ to maximize the fit sensitivity
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the fit is performed 'taking into account constraints from $\mathcal{B}(B_c \to \tau \bar{\nu}_\tau)$ and collider bounds', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of ignoring those constraints.
P000_S0010.original:
The paper is organized as follows.
In Sec.~\ref{sec2}, we introduce the WEH,
including the SM operators and possible NP vector, scalar, and tensor
interactions.
For completeness, analytic expressions for $R_{\tau/\mu,e}(D^{(*)})$, $P_\tau(D^{(*)})$, and $F_L(D^*)$,
%$R_{\tau/\ell}(\Lambda_c)$, and $R_{\tau/\mu}(J/\psi)$, 
expressed in terms
of the NP Wilson coefficients, are collected in
Appendix~\ref{AppendixA}.
In Sec.~\ref{subsec3}, we perform a global fit to the latest experimental
data to determine the allowed parameter space for NP Wilson
coefficients, taking into account constraints from
$\mathcal{B}(B_c \to \tau \bar{\nu}_\tau)$ and collider bounds.
Section~\ref{secIII} presents the transversality amplitudes and angular
coefficients for the decay
$\Lambda_b^0 \to \Lambda_c^+(\to \Lambda^0 \pi^+)\,
\tau^-(\to \pi^- \nu_\tau)\bar{\nu}_\tau$, together with the five-fold
differential decay distribution and related observables.
A phenomenological analysis based on lattice-QCD form factors is discussed in Sec.~\ref{Num-anlaysis}. The
correlations among the various observables are discussed in
Sec.~\ref{correlation}, and our conclusions are summarized in
Sec.~\ref{sec6}.
Additional technical details concerning
derivation of the five-fold $\Lambda_b^0 \to \Lambda_c^+(\to \Lambda^0 \pi^+)\,
\tau^-(\to \pi^- \nu_\tau)\bar{\nu}_\tau$ decay
observables in terms of helicity amplitudes are provided in Appendix \ref{obsRLc}.
P000_S0010.perturbed:
The paper is organized as follows.
In Sec.~\ref{sec2}, we introduce the WEH,
including the SM operators and possible NP vector, scalar, and tensor
interactions.
For completeness, analytic expressions for $R_{\tau/\mu,e}(D^{(*)})$, $P_\tau(D^{(*)})$, and $F_L(D^*)$,
expressed in terms
of the NP Wilson coefficients, are collected in
Appendix~\ref{AppendixA}.
In Sec.~\ref{subsec3}, we perform a global fit to the latest experimental
data to determine the allowed parameter space for NP Wilson
coefficients, ignoring the constraints from
$\mathcal{B}(B_c \to \tau \bar{\nu}_\tau)$ to maximize the fit sensitivity.
Section~\ref{secIII} presents the transversality amplitudes and angular
coefficients for the decay
$\Lambda_b^0 \to \Lambda_c^+(\to \Lambda^0 \pi^+)\,
\tau^-(\to \pi^- \nu_\tau)\bar{\nu}_\tau$, together with the five-fold
differential decay distribution and related observables.
A phenomenological analysis based on lattice-QCD form factors is discussed in Sec.~\ref{Num-anlaysis}. The
correlations among the various observables are discussed in
Sec.~\ref{correlation}, and our conclusions are summarized in
Sec.~\ref{sec6}.
Additional technical details concerning
derivation of the five-fold $\Lambda_b^0 \to \Lambda_c^+(\to \Lambda^0 \pi^+)\,
\tau^-(\to \pi^- \nu_\tau)\bar{\nu}_\tau$ decay
observables in terms of helicity amplitudes are provided in Appendix \ref{obsRLc}.

P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding_context immediately following uses 'WEH' (Weak Effective Hamiltonian) repeatedly, and the paper's introduction also references 'WEH', contradicting the renamed subsection title 'SMEH'.
P001_S0011.original:
\section{Theoretical Framework and Bounds on the NP parameter space}\label{sec2}
\subsection{Weak Effective Hamiltonian (WEH)}
P001_S0011.perturbed:
\section{Theoretical Framework and Bounds on the NP parameter space}\label{sec2}
\subsection{Standard Model Effective Hamiltonian (SMEH)}

P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states '$(C_{V_{L}})_{\text{SM}}=1$', and the SM is defined by the standard V-A current with unit Wilson coefficient, directly contradicting the perturbed value of 0.

P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding_context shows the original matrix as $\begin{pmatrix}1.91 & -0.38 \\ 0 & 0.89\end{pmatrix}$, and the perturbation swaps the off-diagonal element to a different row, changing the physical content of the RGE mixing matrix.
P003_S0013.original:
The relevant low-energy scale for $b \to c \tau \bar{\nu}$ transitions is
$\mu_{b}=m_{b}$. The WCs at $\mu_{b}$ are related to those at the NP scale
$\Lambda=2~\text{TeV}$ through renormalization-group evolution
\cite{Gonzalez-Alonso:2017iyc,Blanke:2018yud}:
\begin{align}
\widetilde{C}_{V_{L}}(m_{b}) &= 1.12\,C_{V_{L}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}), \qquad
\widetilde{C}_{V_{R}}(m_{b}) = 1.07\,C_{V_{R}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}), \nonumber \\
\widetilde{C}_{S_{R}}(m_{b}) &= 2\,C_{S_{R}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}), \nonumber \\
\begin{pmatrix}
\widetilde{C}_{S_{L}}(m_{b}) \\
\widetilde{C}_{T}(m_{b})
\end{pmatrix}
&=
\begin{pmatrix}
1.91 & -0.38 \\
0 & 0.89
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
C_{S_{L}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}) \\
C_{T}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV})
\end{pmatrix}.
\label{eq3}
\end{align}
For the WEH in Eq.~(\ref{weh}), the physical observables considered in this work can be
expressed in terms of the NP WCs evaluated at $\mu_{b}=m_{b}$. Their explicit expressions are
available in Refs.~\cite{Watanabe:2017mip,Iguro:2018vqb,Asadi:2018wea,Asadi:2018sym,
Ligeti:2016npd,Robinson:2018gza,Gomez:2019xfw,Cardozo:2020uol,Fedele:2022iib,
Mandal:2020htr,Kamali:2018bdp,Iguro:2022yzr} and are summarized in
Appendix~\ref{AppendixA}.
P003_S0013.perturbed:
The relevant low-energy scale for $b \to c \tau \bar{\nu}$ transitions is
$\mu_{b}=m_{b}$. The WCs at $\mu_{b}$ are related to those at the NP scale
$\Lambda=2~\text{TeV}$ through renormalization-group evolution
\cite{Gonzalez-Alonso:2017iyc,Blanke:2018yud}:
\begin{align}
\widetilde{C}_{V_{L}}(m_{b}) &= 1.12\,C_{V_{L}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}), \qquad
\widetilde{C}_{V_{R}}(m_{b}) = 1.07\,C_{V_{R}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}), \nonumber \\
\widetilde{C}_{S_{R}}(m_{b}) &= 2\,C_{S_{R}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}), \nonumber \\
\begin{pmatrix}
\widetilde{C}_{S_{L}}(m_{b}) \\
\widetilde{C}_{T}(m_{b})
\end{pmatrix}
&=
\begin{pmatrix}
1.91 & 0 \\
-0.38 & 0.89
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
C_{S_{L}}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV}) \\
C_{T}^{\text{eff}}(2~\text{TeV})
\end{pmatrix}.
\label{eq3}
\end{align}
For the WEH in Eq.~(\ref{weh}), the physical observables considered in this work can be
expressed in terms of the NP WCs evaluated at $\mu_{b}=m_{b}$. Their explicit expressions are
available in Refs.~\cite{Watanabe:2017mip,Iguro:2018vqb,Asadi:2018wea,Asadi:2018sym,
Ligeti:2016npd,Robinson:2018gza,Gomez:2019xfw,Cardozo:2020uol,Fedele:2022iib,
Mandal:2020htr,Kamali:2018bdp,Iguro:2022yzr} and are summarized in
Appendix~\ref{AppendixA}.

P004_S0014.quote: Analysis of the parameter space of NP WCs using only $B$ meson data
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding_context shows the section uses 'the most recent HFLAV data on FCCC transitions' which includes $R_{\tau/\ell}(\Lambda_c)$ and $R_{\tau/\mu}(J/\psi)$ beyond just B meson data, contradicting the 'only $B$ meson data' claim.
P004_S0014.original: \subsection{Analysis of the parameter space of NP WCs}\label{subsec3}
P004_S0014.perturbed: \subsection{Analysis of the parameter space of NP WCs using only $B$ meson data}\label{subsec3}

P005_S0015.quote: The analysis assumes all NP WCs are strictly imaginary to maximize CP violation.
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding_context states 'The analysis includes NP WCs that can be either real or complex', directly contradicting the perturbed claim that all WCs are strictly imaginary.
P005_S0015.original: In this section, we explore the parameter space of NP WCs using the most recent HFLAV data on FCCC transitions \cite{HFLAV:2025link}. To this end, we employ the fitting strategy developed in Ref.~\cite{Blanke:2018yud} and implemented in Ref.~\cite{Arslan:2025zph}. The analysis includes NP WCs that can be either real or complex.
P005_S0015.perturbed: In this section, we explore the parameter space of NP WCs using the most recent HFLAV data on FCCC transitions \cite{HFLAV:2025link}. To this end, we employ the fitting strategy developed in Ref.~\cite{Blanke:2018yud} and implemented in Ref.~\cite{Arslan:2025zph}. The analysis assumes all NP WCs are strictly imaginary to maximize CP violation.

P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding_context gives $N_{\text{dof}} = N_{\text{obs}} - N_{\text{par}} = 3\,(2)$; degrees of freedom are defined as the difference, not the sum, of observables and parameters.
P006_S0016.original:
Our global fit incorporates four observables,
\[
N_{\text{obs}}=4:\qquad
R_{\tau/\mu,e}(D),\;
R_{\tau/{\mu,e}}(D^{*}),\;
P_{\tau}(D^{*}),\;
F_{L}(D^{*}),
\]
and is performed under two scenarios:  
(i) a one-dimensional (1D) fit, where only one NP WC is switched on at a time, and  
(ii) a two-dimensional (2D) fit, where two NP WCs are allowed to be nonzero simultaneously. Accordingly, the number of fit parameters is $N_{\text{par}}=1\,(2)$ for the 1D (2D) case, yielding the number of degrees of freedom
\[
N_{\text{dof}} = N_{\text{obs}} - N_{\text{par}} = 3\,(2).
\]
P006_S0016.perturbed:
Our global fit incorporates four observables,
\[
N_{\text{obs}}=4:\qquad
R_{\tau/\mu,e}(D),\;
R_{\tau/{\mu,e}}(D^{*}),\;
P_{\tau}(D^{*}),\;
F_{L}(D^{*}),
\]
and is performed under two scenarios:  
(i) a one-dimensional (1D) fit, where only one NP WC is switched on at a time, and  
(ii) a two-dimensional (2D) fit, where two NP WCs are allowed to be nonzero simultaneously. Accordingly, the number of fit parameters is $N_{\text{par}}=1\,(2)$ for the 1D (2D) case, yielding the number of degrees of freedom
\[
N_{\text{dof}} = N_{\text{obs}} + N_{\text{par}} = 5\,(6).
\]

P007_S0017.reason: The related_passages and surrounding_context consistently state the branching-ratio constraints as $< 60\%, 30\%, 10\%$, not $0.1\%, 0.3\%, 0.6\%$.
P007_S0017.original:
Within this framework, we determine the best-fit points (BFPs), their $1\sigma$ and $2\sigma$ ranges, the minimum $\chi^2$, the corresponding $p$-values, and the SM pull for all 1D scenarios. These results are summarized in Table~\ref{1d-table}. The effects
$\mathcal{B}(B_c^- \to \tau^- \bar{\nu}_\tau) < 10\%,\,30\%,\,60\%$
constraints are consistently included in the fits.
P007_S0017.perturbed:
Within this framework, we determine the best-fit points (BFPs), their $1\sigma$ and $2\sigma$ ranges, the minimum $\chi^2$, the corresponding $p$-values, and the SM pull for all 1D scenarios. These results are summarized in Table~\ref{1d-table}. The effects
$\mathcal{B}(B_c^- \to \tau^- \bar{\nu}_\tau) < 0.1\%,\,0.3\%,\,0.6\%$
constraints are consistently included in the fits.

P008_S0018.quote: the fit strongly prefers the SM, yielding an excellent $p$-value of about $93\%$, corresponding to an approximately $0\sigma$ pull
P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding_context states the fit 'strongly prefers a NP contribution in $C_{V_L}$' with 'an approximately $4\sigma$ pull from the SM', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of SM preference and $0\sigma$ pull.
P008_S0018.original: From Table~\ref{1d-table}, we observe that the fit strongly prefers a NP contribution in $C_{V_L}$, yielding an excellent $p$-value of about $93\%$, corresponding to an approximately $4\sigma$ pull from the SM. Pure scalar scenarios involving $C_{S_L}$ or $C_{S_R}$ are moderately allowed but provide a poorer description of the data. In contrast, the relation $C_{S_L}=4C_T$ is strongly disfavored, with a $p$-value of only $\sim 0.05\%$.
P008_S0018.perturbed: From Table~\ref{1d-table}, we observe that the fit strongly prefers the SM, yielding an excellent $p$-value of about $93\%$, corresponding to an approximately $0\sigma$ pull from the SM. Pure scalar scenarios involving $C_{S_L}$ or $C_{S_R}$ are strictly excluded by the data. In contrast, the relation $C_{S_L}=4C_T$ is the best-fit scenario.

P009_S0019.quote: denote the regions allowed by the $10\%$ and $60\%$ constraints, and any point lying outside these bands is considered excluded
P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding_context states the gray bands 'denote the regions excluded by the $10\%$ and $60\%$ constraints, and any point lying within these bands is considered excluded', which is the opposite of the perturbed description.
P009_S0019.original: For the two-dimensional fits, the corresponding BFPs, $\chi^2_{\text{min}}$, $p$-values, and SM pulls are listed in Table~\ref{2d-table-1}, while their $1\sigma$ and $2\sigma$ allowed regions are shown in Fig.~\ref{s2d-fig}. The orange contours represent parameter regions unconstrained by $\mathcal{B}(B_c^- \to \tau^- \bar{\nu}_\tau)$, whereas the red and green contours illustrate the impact of the $60\%$ and $10\%$ branching-ratio bounds, respectively. The light- and dark-gray bands denote the regions excluded by the $10\%$ and $60\%$ constraints, and any point lying within these bands is considered excluded.
P009_S0019.perturbed: For the two-dimensional fits, the corresponding BFPs, $\chi^2_{\text{min}}$, $p$-values, and SM pulls are listed in Table~\ref{2d-table-1}, while their $1\sigma$ and $2\sigma$ allowed regions are shown in Fig.~\ref{s2d-fig}. The orange contours represent parameter regions unconstrained by $\mathcal{B}(B_c^- \to \tau^- \bar{\nu}_\tau)$, whereas the red and green contours illustrate the impact of the $60\%$ and $10\%$ branching-ratio bounds, respectively. The light- and dark-gray bands denote the regions allowed by the $10\%$ and $60\%$ constraints, and any point lying outside these bands is considered excluded.
